attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: Personal curation and reflections on podcasts, books, and music that feed the recursive.eco worldview. Migrated from PlayfulProcess Ghost website.

name: Inspiration: Podcasts, Books & Music
description:
A personal library of inspirations — podcasts that shaped my thinking, books that opened doors, and music that holds the soul. Each item carries my own reflection on why it matters. This grammar is a living recommendation list, a map of the voices and works that feed recursive.eco.

Migrated from PlayfulProcess Ghost website (2025).

sections.Note:
I am a Krishnamurti fan in audio form. I love his sense of urgency, the paradoxical fact of an enlightened person and his tone of distress with humanity's ignorance.

For some reason, though, I am not a fan of his writings. Maybe if I become more evolved...
sections.About: Jiddu Krishnamurti (1895-1986) was an Indian philosopher and spiritual teacher who spoke extensively about the nature of the mind, human relationships, and radical transformation. His talks are widely available as podcasts and audio recordings.

sections.Note: There is just so much out there from Alan Watts. But this is a good place to start.
sections.About: Alan Watts (1915-1973) was a British writer and speaker known for interpreting and popularizing Eastern philosophy for a Western audience. His recorded lectures cover Zen Buddhism, Taoism, Hinduism, and the nature of consciousness.
One of the most popular podcasts in the world, featuring long-form interviews with world-class performers across diverse fields.

items.name: The Indie Hackers Podcast & My Path to Authentic Building

sections.Note:
The Indie Hackers podcast is a full-stack community, encompassing entrepreneurs, creators and more. They haven't been producing podcasts openly anymore, but I fully recommend going through their episodes anyway — they're goldmines of real revenue numbers, honest failure stories, and practical strategies from founders who've actually done it.

I would especially recommend the two podcasts with Ghost founder, which is the platform I chose to build my website.
sections.Why Indie Hacking:
My husband was my first serious boyfriend. Instead of trying to research methods to follow other people's paths, I created my own. I decided if I hadn't failed in any relationship before, I would choose to fail this one my own way, learn, and then decide whether to follow others. We have been together for almost 20 years now.

I feel the same about my business. I am doing things differently — I don't have a formal business plan, a clearly defined target audience, or even a traditional 'purpose statement.' But I want to be fully authentic. If I fail, I will learn and iterate.
sections.Ghost: Following Your Own Way:
Ghost represents everything I believe about authentic building. Founded by John O'Nolan, Ghost is a distributed non-profit foundation which gives away all of its intellectual property under a permissive MIT license. The company has no investors and, in fact, no owners of any kind.

They currently generate around $7.5M in annual revenue and have been profitable and sustainable for the past 12 years. Ghost was set up as a non-profit foundation so that it would always be true to its users, rather than shareholders or investors.

items.name: Reach Out and Read: The Leo Lionni Story

sections.Note:
As a national nonprofit organization that promotes early literacy by integrating books into pediatric care, they've been doing this vital work since 1989, now partnering with over 6,000 clinics across all 50 U.S. states.

My favorite episode features my beloved children's book author: Leo Lionni. The conversation with his granddaughter, Annie Lionni, revealed a story that transformed how I understand his work — and perhaps all art born from suppression.
sections.The Real Story: Unfinished Business:
In 1958, Leo Lionni designed the 'Unfinished Business' pavilion for the Brussels World's Fair. Working with the State Department and Henry Luce, Lionni created a bold installation that addressed America's most pressing challenges: urban decay, environmental degradation, and most controversially, racial segregation.

Within weeks of opening, Southern senators erupted. The exhibit was shut down. Lionni was made the scapegoat for what was clearly a political decision.
sections.From Suppression to Liberation:
Annie Lionni's revelation is stunning: 'Leo never said this, but I think all of his children's books came from the shutting down of the pavilion.'

Just six months after the pavilion's closure, on that train ride with his grandchildren, Lionni spontaneously created Little Blue and Little Yellow. The message that politicians silenced for adults, Lionni reimagined for children — those who would shape the future.
sections.A Legacy: Lionni went on to create more than 40 children's books, four winning Caldecott Honors. His stories consistently explored themes of community, tolerance, and maintaining oneself in the face of pressure. The censorship didn't end with Brussels. As recently as 2015, the mayor of Venice banned several Lionni titles for 'potentially threatening traditional families.'

sections.Note:
The Power of Now felt like my first spiritual initiation — though I didn't know it at the time. I first read it at sixteen, and I've kept returning to it ever since. Everything I've come to believe about mindfulness, psychology, spirituality, and even creativity seems to live in its pages. Yet Eckhart Tolle expresses these truths with a simplicity that cuts through complexity.

I dream of creating children's books that gently introduce ideas like these — alongside others from Tolle's teachings, such as the pain body. I want to find a playful, effective way to help kids see that they are not their emotions — and that they can ride out a temper tantrum like a wave. Wish me luck!
sections.About: A guide to spiritual enlightenment through present-moment awareness, by Eckhart Tolle. One of the most influential spiritual books of the modern era.

items.name: The Short Path to Enlightenment (Paul Brunton)

sections.Note:
In Paul Brunton's The Short Path to Enlightenment, he speaks of a direct realization — the awakening to the ever-present truth of our essential nature. Rather than slowly refining the ego through discipline and practice, the Short Path invites us to leap into Being, recognizing that what we seek has never been absent.

In our PlayfulProcess terms: The triangle — Feelings, Thoughts, Behaviors — represents the Long Path, the traditional route of purification, discipline, and healing. But hovering above is the sun of Being — radiant, changeless, always now. The Short Path is not a denial of the triangle; it is a shift in identity. You realize that you are not the triangle. You are the light that sees it.

Brunton reminds us that what you are looking for is what is looking.
sections.About: Paul Brunton (1898-1981) was a British philosopher, mystic, and traveler who introduced Indian philosophy to the West. The Short Path emphasizes direct recognition of one's essential nature rather than gradual self-improvement.

sections.Note:
The Tao Te Ching offers us a wisdom that feels like sunlight: gentle, ever-present, and beyond grasp. Laozi speaks not of striving, fixing, or earning, but of returning — to the Tao, the Way that flows through all things.

In our PlayfulProcess terms: The triangle — Thoughts, Feelings, Behaviors — is the realm of form, of personality, of the conditioned self. Above the triangle is the sun of Being — the Tao itself. You cannot reach it through effort. The Short Path in Taoist terms is wu wei — non-doing.

You don't become the sun by improving the triangle. You remember you are already light — quiet, spacious, always now.
sections.About: The foundational text of Taoism, attributed to Lao Tzu (6th century BCE). 81 verses on the nature of the Tao, wu wei (non-action), and the art of living in harmony with the Way.

sections.Note:
Effortless is the most pragmatic way I've found to live by presence. If The Power of Now opened my heart to the mystery of being, Effortless helped bring that presence into everyday action. Greg McKeown shows that ease isn't laziness — it's alignment.

In PlayfulProcess terms, Effortless is about letting the light of Being shine through our everyday actions — without resistance. When we let go of the internal noise — the mental clutter, the guilt-driven shoulds, the productivity-at-all-costs mindset — we make space.

This book is a gift for anyone who's trying too hard. Especially for creators, parents, and sensitive souls — it offers a new kind of discipline: the discipline of allowing.

Just like I feel he translated spiritual truths to business, I intend to do the same with books for children.
sections.About: By Greg McKeown, author of Essentialism. A practical guide to making the most important things easier, not harder. Combines productivity wisdom with a deeper philosophy of alignment and flow.

items.name: Little Blue and Little Yellow (Leo Lionni)

sections.Note:
In this groundbreaking work of children's literature, Little Blue and Little Yellow are best friends who love to play together. One day, they hug each other so tightly that they merge into green. When they return home, neither of their families recognizes them.

What appears to be a simple story about colors and friendship carries a profound historical weight. Created just six months after Leo Lionni's 'Unfinished Business' pavilion at the 1958 Brussels World's Fair was shut down by Southern senators for depicting racial integration, this book was Lionni's response to censorship.

The pavilion had featured a photograph of seven racially diverse children playing Ring Around the Rosie. In Little Blue and Little Yellow, seven colored dots play the same game. The message that politicians silenced for adults, Lionni reimagined for children.
sections.Purpose: He believed that 'the audience he most wanted to write for was the audience who would make the future.' His commitment to children wasn't sentimental — it was revolutionary.
sections.A Living Legacy: Today, Lionni's work continues to spark conversations about friendship, family, identity, and acceptance. That his work has been banned as recently as 2015 (in Venice, for 'threatening traditional families') proves that Lionni's 'unfinished business' remains unfinished.

sections.Note:
The Hero's Journey isn't just for epic fantasies; it lives in the gentle pages of picture books and the determined hearts of young readers discovering their own courage.

Children live the Hero's Journey every day. They face dragons in the form of first days at school, navigate unknown worlds when families move, and discover magical helpers in unexpected friends. These archetypal stories matter because they give children a roadmap for their own transformations.

The pattern is timeless: A young person feels different or inadequate. They venture into unfamiliar territory. They face challenges that seem insurmountable. Through persistence, creativity, and often the help of others, they discover strengths they didn't know they had.
sections.About: A curated collection of children's books that embody Joseph Campbell's Hero's Journey pattern, by several authors.

sections.Note: A heartfelt story about a child named Taylor whose carefully built block tower is destroyed. A series of animals offer distractions and advice — until the rabbit arrives and simply listens. This gentle act helps Taylor process sadness and find comfort in being heard.
sections.Why It Teaches Empathy:
Validates feelings — The narrative acknowledges Taylor's emotions of sadness, disappointment, and even grief, without trivializing them.

Highlights deep listening — The rabbit doesn't try to fix things; it allows space for Taylor to feel, modeling an essential empathetic practice.

Appeals to all ages — Reviews praise the spare, poignant language and inclusive, gender-neutral illustrations, noting it resonates with toddlers and adults alike.

Supports emotional learning — Educators recommend it as a starting point for group discussions about grief, frustration, and emotional support.

sections.Note:
Obaluaye, also known as Omolu, is an Orisha in the Yoruba tradition associated with illness, healing, and transformation. He governs diseases but is also a powerful force of purification and renewal.

I created this playlist at the beginning of the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic as a form of spiritual and emotional grounding.
One of the best contemporary albums in Brazilian music.

Tribalistas is an award-winning Brazilian supergroup whose music blends soulful harmonies, poetic lyrics, and Afro-Brazilian rhythms. Formed by Marisa Monte, Arnaldo Antunes, and Carlinhos Brown, their sound is both timeless and fresh — I never get tired of listening to them. Their songs feel like a warm conversation you can return to again and again.

The Grammy-winning duo Joe Mailander and Justin Lansing create Americana and folk music that resonates with both children and adults. Their songs explore nature, big feelings, and family life — always with sincerity and never talking down to kids. Their 2023 album Brambletown weaves together themes of community, the environment, love, and resilience in a way that's thoughtful, imaginative, and truly multigenerational.

sections.About: Voices that shaped my thinking. From Krishnamurti's urgent presence to the indie hacker ethos of building authentically, these podcasts represent the range of influences that feed recursive.eco — spiritual, entrepreneurial, and literary.
sections.How to Use: Browse these when you want to hear the original voices behind the ideas. Each recommendation comes with my personal note on why it matters to me.

sections.About: Books that opened doors. Some are spiritual (Tolle, Brunton, Lao Tzu), some are practical (McKeown), and some are children's picture books that carry revolutionary messages (Lionni, Doerrfeld). Each review includes my personal PlayfulProcess framing.
sections.How to Use: Start with whichever resonates. The spiritual books connect to each other through the 'triangle and sun' metaphor. The children's books connect to the kids grammars on recursive.eco.

sections.About: Music that holds the soul. Brazilian roots (Tribalistas, Acorda Amor), Yoruba sacred tradition (Obaluaye), and multigenerational folk (Okee Dokee Brothers). Each playlist carries a story about when and why it was made.
sections.How to Use: Listen. These aren't background music — each one was curated at a specific moment in life, for a specific emotional need.
